elements.sent-2411-001: Neural network force field models such as DeePMD have enabled highly efficient large-scale molecular dynamics simulations with ab initio accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, building such models heavily depends on the training data obtained by costly electronic structure calculations, thereby it is crucial to carefully select and label the most representative configurations during model training to improve both extrapolation capability and training efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address this challenge, based on the Kalman filter theory we propose the Kalman Prediction Uncertainty (KPU) to quantify uncertainty of the model's prediction.
elements.sent-2411-004: With KPU we design the Active Learning by KPU (ALKPU) method, which can efficiently select representative configurations that should be labelled during model training.
elements.sent-2411-005: We prove that ALKPU locally leads to the fastest reduction of model's uncertainty, which reveals its rationality as a general active learning method.
elements.sent-2411-006: We test the ALKPU method using various physical system simulations and demonstrate that it can efficiently coverage the system's configuration space.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our work demonstrates the benefits of ALKPU as a novel active learning method, enhancing training efficiency and reducing computational resource demands.
elements.sent-2411-008: The ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) is a powerful computational tool that allows the simulation of dynamical processes involving atoms and molecules to gain a detailed understanding of physical and chemical properties of complex systems with quantum mechanics precision, such as chemical bonding and formation of reaction intermediates for metal alloys, ceramics and polymers \cite{nose1984,323_1985Computer,development1996,raty2005growth,aminpour2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: However, AIMD is computationally expensive due to the need for electronic structure calculations to obtain the energy and forces of the system at each step, which limits the scale of the simulated system and time duration \cite{PhysRevLett.55.2471,marx_hutter_2009,pwmat1 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Recently, the deep learning methods provide tools to combine traditional AIMD with a neural network (NN) to develop neural network force field (NNFF) \cite{blank1995neural,2007Generalized,doi:10.1063/1.3553717,behler2021four , in which an NN model is designed that maps configurations of the simulated system to their respective potential energies and forces.}
elements.sent-2411-011: The well-trained NNFF model can be used to rapidly generate energies and forces to drive molecular dynamics (MD), improving the speed and scale of simulations while maintaining ab initio accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-012: This approach has been effectively used for simulating complex condensed-phase systems as well as molecular transport studies \cite{Representing2004,2019Neural,friederich2021machine .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The performance of an NNFF depends heavily on the quality of training data generated by electronic structure calculation softwares such as VASP and PWmat \cite{hutchinson2012vasp,pwmat1,pwmat2 , and efficient sampling strategies play a crucial role in ensuring that the model is accurate and reliable across the entire range of configurations of interest \cite{2020exploring .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Thus, it is imperative to find such a method that selects informative and representative configurations to build a relative small training dataset with which we can train a model with the desired performance \cite{smith2018less,zhang2019active .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Active learning is a technique that concurrently selects the most informative data samples for labeling during training, which can significantly reduce the labeling effort and save much training time required to reach a given level of performance.
elements.sent-2411-016: Many NNFF models and packages have been developed, such as HDNNP~\cite{Behler_2014, doi:10.1063/1.3553717, behler2017first , BIM-NN~\cite{doi:10.1021/acs.jpclett.7b01072 , Schnet~\cite{Schutt2017 , Enn-s2s~\cite{gilmer2017neural , DeepMD-kit~\cite{wang2018deepmd , SIMPLE-NN~\cite{lee2019simple , DimeNet++~\cite{gasteiger_dimenet_2020, gasteiger_dimenetpp_2020 , PaiNN~\cite{schutt2021equivariant , SpookyNet~\cite{Unke2021 , CabanaMD-NNP \cite{DESAI2022108156 , NewtonNet~\cite{haghighatlari2022newtonnet and NequIP~\cite{Batzner2022 .}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: The Deep Potential Molecular Dynamics (DeePMD) is one of the state-of-the-art model among them \cite{zhang2018deep,wang2018deepmd,zhang2018end,jia2020pushing .}
elements.sent-2411-018: It contains an embedding network to learning symmetric invariant features \cite{doi:10.1063/1.3553717,PhysRevB.87.184115 from input configurations and a fitting network to map the feature to potential energies and forces \cite{zhang2018end .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: The model is trained using the Adam optimizer \cite{2014Adam and an active learning method called DP-GEN is designed to select the most relevant portions of the high-dimensional configuration space \cite{zhang2020dp .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: DP-GEN has two main parts: exploration and selection.
elements.sent-2411-021: Exploration involves using the temporary trained model to run several steps MD, while selection selects representative configurations from this MD trajectory that should be labeled to enlarge the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-022: DP-GEN trains four models with different initial NN parameters, then uses the predictions of the trained model to calculate an empirical variance, and selects data points with high variances.
elements.sent-2411-023: Although it has been applied to many physical system simulations, DP-GEN has two main problems.
elements.sent-2411-024: Firstly, it incurs substantial computational expense due to the training of four models.
elements.sent-2411-025: Secondly, there is a consistency-error problem when using multiple models to calculate variance, which means that all four trained models may make consistently incorrect predictions for a given configuration \cite{zhang2020machine , potentially leading to unnecessary redundant or missed acquisitions of configurations that the model has not covered.}
elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper, based on the RLEKF optimizer \cite{hu2022rlekf that exploits the extended Kalman filter (EKF) \cite{kalman1960contributions,KF155276,KF728124,haykin2001kalman,chui2017kalman to train DeePMD, we develop a new active learning method.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: By taking advantages of the Kalman filter's capabilities of estimating the probability distribution of NN parameters, we propose a metric to quantify uncertainty of the model's prediction called the Kalman Prediction Uncertainty (KPU), which is an approximate variance of the model's prediction.
elements.sent-2411-028: In the framework of the RLEKF optimizer, we propose an efficient algorithm to calculate KPU, then with KPU we design the Active Learning by KPU (ALKPU) method for DeePMD.
elements.sent-2411-029: After the exploration step, ALKPU computes the KPU for each configuration in the new MD trajectory, and configurations with higher KPUs are selected for labeling and then added to the training dataset concurrently during model training.
elements.sent-2411-030: Comparing with DP-GEN's four-model method, ALKPU is more appropriate to quantify uncertainty of the model's prediction and trains only one model, thus can save large computational resource overheads while lead to a better selection of representative data points.
elements.sent-2411-031: The main contributions of this work are summarized in the following.
elements.sent-2411-032: Based on the EKF for training NN, we propose the KPU to quantify uncertainty of the NN's predictions.
elements.sent-2411-033: In the framework of the RLEKF optimizer for training DeePMD, we design an efficient algorithm to calculate the KPU of forces predicted by the temporary trained model.
elements.sent-2411-034: We propose the ALKPU active learning method for DeePMD and develop a sampling platform that can concurrently exploring, selecting and labeling representative configurations during training.
elements.sent-2411-035: Several typical physical system are used to test the ALKPU method and show its advantages as a new active learning method.
elements.sent-2411-036: We establish some theoretical results about ALKPU with mathematical proofs by using the Fisher information matrix and Shannon entropy.
elements.sent-2411-037: The results show that ALKPU is essentially a locally fastest reduction procedure of NN model's uncertainty, which reveals the rationality of ALKPU as a general active learning method.
elements.sent-2411-038: Active learning is a widely studied topic, with early work found in \cite{2010Active .}
elements.sent-2411-039: The center of active learning is the query strategy that decides which data points are informative and representative that should be selected and labelled \cite{angluin2001queries .}
elements.sent-2411-040: DP-GEN belongs to the ``query by committee'' family where a variety of models are trained on the current labeled data and then votes on the output for unlabeled data and labels those points for which the ``committee'' disagrees the most \cite{seung1992query .}
elements.sent-2411-041: ALKPU belongs to the ``uncertainty sampling'' family that labels those points for which the current model is the least certain about regarding the correct output \cite{Faria2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Other query strategies include the variance reduction that labels points that would minimize output variance \cite{mackay1992information,cohn1993neural,cohn1996active , the expected error reduction that label points that would mostly reduce the model's generalization error \cite{roy2001toward and the expected model change that labels points that would mostly change the current model \cite{settles2007multiple .}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Statistical learning methods such as the Gaussian regression process and Bayesian neural network can give better uncertainty measures \cite{rasmussen2006gaussian,deringer2021gaussian , but it is hard to directly applied them to deep neural networks.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Recent research \cite{gal2016dropout,li2017dropout,gal2017concrete has demonstrated connections between the dropout based active learning and approximate Bayesian inference, making it possible to apply Bayesian active learning methods to deep learning.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Active learning is particularly useful for data-extensive scenarios such as chemical-space exploration in MD simulations.
elements.sent-2411-046: One effective algorithm proposed in \cite{botu2015adaptive to actively learn energies and forces for MD of bulk aluminium and aluminium surfaces employs Euclidean distance between configurations as a selection criterion.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The ``query by committee'' was applied first for NNFFs in \cite{behler2015constructing where a committee of NN models with different architectures but comparable average performances is selected.}
elements.sent-2411-048: In contrast to NNs, in \cite{simm2018error the prediction variance of Gaussian regression process was used to learn complex chemical reaction mechanisms.}
elements.sent-2411-049: This approach was further extended to enable the generation of multiple labels simultaneously, providing a significant acceleration of matter simulations \cite{proppe2019gaussian .}
elements.sent-2411-050: For the linearly parametrized interatomic potential \cite{podryabinkin2017active , an active learning approach using the D-optimality criterion for selecting configurations was designed.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Additionally, active learning approaches have been applied to accelerate chemical-space exploration based on the Thompson sampling \cite{hernandez2017parallel and to speed up exploration of potential energy surfaces by synthesizing representations of unseen configurations \cite{gomez2018automatic .}}

elements.sent-2411-052: The state-of-the-art NNFF used in this work is the DeePMD model \cite{zhang2018deep,zhang2018end .}

elements.sent-2411-070: The idea of exploiting EKF to train NN is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-071: Let $\boldsymbol{w}$ denotes the vector including all the well-trained parameters.
elements.sent-2411-072: The NN model can be modelled as \begin{equation}\begin{cases} \boldsymbol{v}_{t}=\boldsymbol{v}_{t-1}=\boldsymbol{w}, y_{t}=h(\boldsymbol{v}_{t},x_{t})+\eta_{t}, \end{cases}\end{equation} where $\{(x_{t ,y_{t )\}_{t\in \mathbb{N }$ are training data pairs, $h(\cdot, \cdot)$ is the nonlinear NN mapping, and $\eta_{t}$ is the noise for the NN model.}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: In order to avoid overfitting of earlier data, a fading memory strategy is used, which leads to the following model: \begin{equation}\label{fade} \begin{cases} \boldsymbol{v}_{t}=\lambda_{t}^{-1/2}\boldsymbol{v}_{t-1}, \ \ \ \boldsymbol{v}_{0} \sim \mathcal{N}(\hat{\boldsymbol{w}}_0, P_0), y_{t}=h(\alpha_{t}^{-1}\boldsymbol{v}_{t},x_{t})+\eta_{t}, \ \ \ \eta_{t}\sim{\mathcal {N}} (0, R_{t}), \end{cases}\end{equation} where $0< \lambda_{i}\leq 1$ are called fading memory factors, $\boldsymbol{v _{0 =\boldsymbol{w}$ and $\mathcal{N (\hat{\boldsymbol{w _0, P_0)$ is the initial distribution of $\boldsymbol{w}$.}}}}}

elements.section-2411-003: Active learning by Kalman Prediction Uncertainty
elements.sent-2411-079: Active learning minimizes labeling costs while maximizing modeling accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-080: While there are various methods in active learning literature, we propose to label the data point whose model uncertainty is the highest.
elements.sent-2411-081: Thanks to the EKF that estimates the distribution of trained NN parameter, we can estimate the variance around the model's prediction and use it as an uncertainty measure.
elements.sent-2411-082: After $t$ iterations of training by EKFfm, we have estimated parameter $\boldsymbol{w}$ with expectation $\hat{\boldsymbol{w _t$ and covariance $\hat{P _t=\alpha_{t ^{-2 P_t$ due to $\hat{\boldsymbol{w _t=\alpha_{t ^{-1}\hat{\boldsymbol{v _{t}$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: If we treat the $t$-th trained $\boldsymbol{w}$ as Gaussian $\boldsymbol{w}\sim\mathcal{N (\hat{\boldsymbol{w _t,P_t)$ and approximate $h(\boldsymbol{w ,x)$ near $\hat{\boldsymbol{w _t$ by the one-order Taylor expansion $h(\boldsymbol{w ,x)\approx h(\hat{\boldsymbol{w _t,x)+D_{\boldsymbol{w h(\hat{\boldsymbol{w _t,x)(\boldsymbol{w -\hat{\boldsymbol{w _t)$, then $h(\boldsymbol{w ,x)$ approximately obeys the distribution \begin{equation}N(h(\hat{w}_t,x), D_{w}h(\hat{w}_t,x)\hat{P}_{t}D_{w}h(\hat{w}_t,x)^T)= N(h(\hat{w}_t,x), \alpha_{t}^{-2}D_{w}h(\hat{w}_t,x)P_{t}D_{w}h(\hat{w}_t,x)^T) .\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: By neglecting the scalar factor $\alpha_{t ^{-2}$ at each iteration, we define the Kalman Prediction Uncertainty (KPU) as follows to quantity uncertainty of the model's prediction for an input $x$.}
elements.sent-2411-085: When the prediction has dimension 1, the KPU becomes $\mathrm{KPU _t(x) = \nabla_{\boldsymbol{w h(\hat{\boldsymbol{w _t,x)^{T P_{t}\nabla_{\boldsymbol{w h(\hat{\boldsymbol{w _t,x)$ that is a nonnegative number.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: For the RLEKF optimizer, the computed $P_t$ has a block diagonal form corresponding to the reorganized layer strategy, thus KPU can be calculated as Algorithm \ref{KPU_layer .}
elements.sent-2411-088: Since RLEKF is a block diagonal approximation to EKFfm, the computed KPU in RLEKF is an approximation to the real KPU defined in \eqref{KPU .}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{KPU calculation in RLEKF ($\mathtt{KPU\_cal}$)}\label{KPU_layer} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require $\hat{\boldsymbol{w}}_{t}$, $P_t=\mathrm{diag}(P_{t1},\dots,P_{tL})$, input $x$ \Comment{$P_t$ is block diagonal} \State $H = \mathrm{D}_{\boldsymbol{w}}h(\hat{\boldsymbol{w}}_{t}, x)$ \Comment{Backward propagation} \State Split $H$ corresponding to $P_t$: $\{H_1, \dots, H_L\}=\mathtt{split}(H)$ \For{$l =1,\dots, L$} \Comment{$L$ is the number of reorganized layers} \State $ KPU_l = H_{l}P_{tl}H_{l}^{T} $\Comment{KPU for each reorganized layer} \EndFor \Ensure $\mathrm{KPU}_t(x)=\sum_{l=1}^{L}KPU_l$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{KPU for atomic force ($\mathtt{KPU_{force}}$)}\label{KPU_cal} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require $\hat{\boldsymbol{w}}_t$, $P_t$, configuration $\mathcal{R}=\{\mathcal{R}_{i}\}_{i=1}^{N_a}$ \State $ E = \sum_{i} h_E(\hat{\boldsymbol{w}}_{t}, \mathcal{R}_{i})$ \Comment{Predicted total energy} \State $\boldsymbol{F}_{i}=-\nabla_{\mathbf{r}_{i}}E$ \Comment{Predicted atomic force} \State $\{\boldsymbol{F}_1,\dots,\boldsymbol{F}_n\}=\mathtt{rand\_select}(N_a,\mathtt{frac})$\Comment{Randomly select $n=\lfloor N_a\cdot \mathtt{frac}\rfloor$ atoms} \For{$j =1,\dots,n$} \For{$k = 1, 2, 3$} \State $KPU_{F_{jk}}=\mathtt{KPU\_cal}(\hat{\boldsymbol{w}}_{t}, P_{t}, \mathcal{R}; F_{jk})$ \Comment{KPU for force component $F_{jk}$} \EndFor \EndFor \Ensure $\mathrm{KPU}_{\mathrm{force}}= \sum_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{3}KPU_{F_{jk}}/3n$ \Comment{Average of KPU for forces} \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-094: During model training, the active learning by KPU (ALKPU) method concurrently selects the next data points with higher KPUs.
elements.sent-2411-095: This gives the following procedure of ALKPU for DeePMD:.
elements.sent-2411-096: With the initial training dataset, a temporary model is trained by RLEKF.
elements.sent-2411-097: Then several steps of MD is run using the energy and forces generated by this NN model.
elements.sent-2411-098: This procedure is used to explore the high-dimensional configuration space to obtain some configurations not too far away from the real AIMD trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-099: For the configurations obtained by the exploration procedure, we need to select the most representative ones.
elements.sent-2411-100: The $\mathtt{KPU_{force }$ of each configuration is computed and then judged whether $\sigma_0\leq \mathtt{KPU_{force }\leq \sigma_1$ with $\sigma_0=c_0\mathrm{KPU _{\mathrm{trained }$ and $\sigma_1=c_1\mathrm{KPU _{\mathrm{trained }$, where $\mathrm{KPU _{\mathrm{trained }$ is the average KPU of some training data at the last training step.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: The threshold $\sigma_0$ is used to select configurations with higher KPUs that needed to be labelled.
elements.sent-2411-102: If the KPU is higher than $\sigma_1$, it means that this configuration deviates too far from the real AIMD trajectory and thus it should be discarded.
elements.sent-2411-103: For those selected configurations, labelling can be done by using an electronic structure calculation software to compute the corresponding total energy and atomic forces.
elements.sent-2411-104: The above labelled data are added to the previous training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-105: With the enlarged training dataset, the model is trained again.
elements.sent-2411-106: This cycle can be repeated multiple times until a well-trained model with desired performance is obtained.
elements.sent-2411-107: The workflow of the whole procedure is shown in Figure \ref{fig1 .}

elements.sent-2411-182: Overall, the test results indicate that ALKPU can effectively select representative data points to cover the configuration space during training.
elements.sent-2411-183: It has great potential for improving training efficiency to build a final usable model and can significantly accelerate MD simulations while maintaining ab initio accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-184: In our future work, the ALKPU method will be tested on more complex systems with much larger scales to further explore its capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-185: To enhance the extrapolation capability and improve training efficiency for DeePMD, we have proposed ALKPU, an active learning method that can concurrently select representative configurations for labelling during model training.
elements.sent-2411-186: We developed an efficient algorithm to calculate KPU for atomic forces and an ALKPU sampling platform that concurrently explores, selects, and labels representative configurations during training.
elements.sent-2411-187: We have shown that ALKPU is essentially a locally fastest reduction procedure of model's uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-188: We test the ALKPU method with various physical system simulations and compare it with DeePMD's original DP-GEN active learning method.
elements.sent-2411-189: Our results show that ALKPU improves training efficiency and reduces computational overhead, offering significant advantages as a new active learning approach.
elements.sent-2411-190: Although ALKPU is designed for DeePMD, KPU is a general metric for quantifying uncertainty in NN predictions.
elements.sent-2411-191: When combined with an efficient Kalman filter-based optimizer, ALKPU can be applied to other "AI for Science" problems where model training is time-consuming and acquiring training data is computationally expensive.. .

elements.sent-2411-192: We first review the basic algorithm of the extended Kalman filter (EKF) and then derive the EKFfm algorithm.

elements.sent-2411-194: Write a discrete stochastic dynamical system with observations as \begin{equation}\label{DSD}  s_{t}=f(s_{t-1}, u_t)+\xi_t, \ \ s_0 \sim N(s_0, P_0), y_{t}=h(s_{t}, u_t)+\eta_{t}, \ \ j=1,2\dots \end{equation} where $f\in C^1(\mathbb{R ^n, \mathbb{R ^n)$, $h\in C^1(\mathbb{R ^n, \mathbb{R ^m)$ and $\xi_t\sim\mathcal{N (0,Q_t)$, $\eta_t\sim\mathcal{N (0,R_t)$ are noises.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-196: The Kalman filter is an iterative algorithm that estimates the distribution $\mathbb{P}\left(s_t|\{y_i\}_{i=1 ^{i=t}\right)$ by computing its expectation and covariance \cite{kalman1960contributions,chui2017kalman .}}
elements.sent-2411-197: For nonlinear mappings $f$ and $g$, the EKF applies the linear Kalman filter to a linear Gaussian system approximated by using one-order Taylor expansion, and the output is an approximation to the real distribution of $\mathbb{P}\left(s_t|\{y_i\}_{i=1 ^{t}\right)$.}

elements.sent-2411-218: At the $t$-th step, the energy process fits the predicted total energy to the labeled energy of configuration $\mathcal{R ^{(t)}$ by nonlinear NN mapping $h_{E}$, while the force process fits the predicted atom forces to the labeled forces by nonlinear mapping $h_{F}$ that is the gradient of $h_{E}$ with respect to coordinates.}
elements.sent-2411-219: During the force process, a set of $n_a$ atoms is randomly chosen from the configuration and the corresponding force vectors are concatenated into a scalar (i.e. lines 1--6 of Algorithm \ref{RLEKF2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-220: This process (lines 4--5) is repeated $n_F$ times in one step.
elements.sent-2411-221: In the practical implementation we set $n_a=6$ and $n_F=4$.
elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{High-level structure of training by RLEKF}\label{RLEKF} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require $\hat{\boldsymbol{w}}_0$, $P_0$, $\lambda_1$, $\nu$, training data $\{\mathcal{R}^{(t)}, E_{\text{DFT}}^{(t)}, \{\boldsymbol{F}_{i,\text{DFT}}^{(t)}\}\}_{t\in\mathbb{N}}$ \For{$t =1, 2,\dots,T$} \State $E = h_E(\hat{\boldsymbol{w}}_{t-1}, \mathcal{R}^{(t)})$ \Comment{Predicted total energy} \State $\hat{\boldsymbol{w}}_{+}, P_{+}, \lambda_{+} =\mathtt{RLEKF}(E, E_{\text{DFT}}^{(t)}, \hat{\boldsymbol{w}}_{t-1}, \mathbf{P}_{t-1}, \lambda_{t})$ \Comment{Training by energy} \State $\{\boldsymbol{F}_{i}\} = h_{F}(\hat{\boldsymbol{w}}_{+}, \mathcal{R}^{(t)})$ \Comment{Predicted forces} \State $\hat{\boldsymbol{w}}_{t}, P_{t}, \lambda_{t+1} =\mathtt{RLEKF}(\{\boldsymbol{F}_{i}\}, \{\boldsymbol{F}_{i,\text{DFT}}^{(t)}\}, w_{+}, \mathbf{P}_{+}, \lambda_{+})$ \Comment{Training by forces} \EndFor \Ensure $\hat{\boldsymbol{w}}_T$, $P_T$, $\lambda_{T+1}$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.section-2411-008: Shannon entropy of the multivariate Gaussian distribution
elements.sent-2411-222: A vector-valued random variable $X=[X_1, \dots, X_n]^T\in\mathbb{R ^n$ is said to have a multivariate Gaussian distribution if its probability density function is given by \begin{equation}p(x) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2}(\det\Sigma)^{1/2}}\exp{\Big(-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^{T}\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)\Big)},\end{equation} with the expectation (or mean) $\mu\in\mathbb{R ^{n}$ and covariance $\Sigma\in\mathbb{R ^{n\times n}$ where $\Sigma$ is semi-definite.}}}
elements.sent-2411-224: The Shannon entropy of $X$ can be calculated as follows.. First, \begin{equation}H(X) = -\int_{R^n}p(x)\ln p(x)dx = -\int_{R^n}[\ln((2\pi)^{n/2}(\det\Sigma)^{1/2})+ \frac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^{T}\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)]p(x)dx = \frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi)+\frac{1}{2}\ln(\det \Sigma)+\frac{1}{2}E_{x\sim p(x)}[(x-\mu)^{T}\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-226: Thus we have \begin{equation}E_{x\sim p(x)}[(x-\mu)^{T}\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)] = E_{x\sim p(x)}[tr((x-\mu)^{T}\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu))] = E_{x\sim p(x)}[tr(\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)(x-\mu)^{T})] = tr(\Sigma^{-1}E_{x\sim p(x)}[(x-\mu)(x-\mu)^{T}]) = tr(\Sigma^{-1}\Sigma) = n.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-028: \begin{equation}E_{x\sim p(x)}[(x-\mu)^{T}\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)] = E_{x\sim p(x)}[tr((x-\mu)^{T}\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu))] = E_{x\sim p(x)}[tr(\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)(x-\mu)^{T})] = tr(\Sigma^{-1}E_{x\sim p(x)}[(x-\mu)(x-\mu)^{T}]) = tr(\Sigma^{-1}\Sigma) = n.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-228: Note that for the $n$ dimensional Gaussian random vectors, the Shannon entropy depends only on its covariance but not its mean.
